title: BC Property Transfer Tax: The 2026 Buyer's Guide
metaTitle: BC Property Transfer Tax Guide 2026 — Vancouver Buyers
metaDescription: Complete guide to BC's Property Transfer Tax in 2026. Tiered rates, first-time buyer exemption, new build exemption, and the 20% Additional PTT for foreign buyers.
excerpt: BC's Property Transfer Tax catches most Greater Vancouver buyers off guard. Here's exactly what you'll owe — and which exemptions might apply.

body.heading: How PTT is calculated
paragraphs: BC charges Property Transfer Tax on the fair market value of residential property you buy. The rates are tiered: 1% on the first $200,000, 2% on the portion from $200,000 to $2,000,000, 3% on the portion from $2M to $3M, and an additional 2% (5% total) on the residential value above $3M.

On a $950,000 Burnaby townhouse: 1% × $200,000 + 2% × $750,000 = $17,000 in PTT. On a $1.6M North Vancouver home: 1% × $200,000 + 2% × $1,400,000 = $30,000. PTT must be paid out of closing funds — it can't be financed into the mortgage.

body.heading: First-time buyer exemption
paragraphs: Eligible first-time buyers get full PTT exemption on properties up to $500,000 of fair market value, with a partial exemption phasing out up to $835,000. To qualify: be a Canadian citizen or permanent resident, have lived in BC for the past 12 months or filed BC income tax for the past 6 years, never have owned a principal residence anywhere in the world, and occupy the home as your principal residence within 92 days. The exemption typically saves $8,000–$16,000 on a Greater Vancouver first-time purchase.

body.heading: New build exemption
paragraphs: Newly constructed homes purchased as principal residences qualify for full PTT exemption up to $1,100,000, with partial exemption up to $1,150,000. Useful for buyers of pre-sale condos in Burnaby, Surrey and Vancouver. The exemption stacks with first-time buyer status if you also qualify there.

body.heading: 20% Additional Property Transfer Tax
paragraphs: Foreign buyers — anyone who is not a Canadian citizen or permanent resident — pay an additional 20% PTT on residential property in Metro Vancouver, the Fraser Valley, the Capital Regional District, the Central Okanagan, and Greater Nanaimo. This is on top of the regular tiered PTT. On a $1.5M West Vancouver home, that's $300,000 of additional tax — usually a deal-breaker for non-resident purchases.

body.heading: Don't confuse PTT with annual taxes
paragraphs: PTT is a one-time closing-cost tax. The Speculation and Vacancy Tax (provincial), Empty Homes Tax (City of Vancouver), and Underused Housing Tax (federal) are annual taxes that affect non-occupant or vacant properties. They don't apply at closing — they apply yearly thereafter.

faqs.question: Is PTT financeable?
faqs.answer: No. PTT must be paid out of your closing funds — typically as part of the lawyer's closing statement. Plan for it as part of your down-payment-and-closing-costs budget.

faqs.question: Do all closing costs go into the mortgage?
faqs.answer: Generally, no. The mortgage funds the purchase price minus your down payment. Closing costs — PTT, legal fees, adjustments, title insurance — are paid separately at closing. Budget 1.5–4% of purchase price for closing costs depending on whether you qualify for PTT exemptions.
